story:
Brad stumbled through the front door every night, arms full of overstuffed manila folders, leaving before dawn, returning after dark, and working again after a quick dinner and a tired peck on Cathy’s cheek. He looked thinner in his crumpled gray suit and walked with a kind of stoop, as if responsibility itself weighed him down. Cathy worried and bit back the urge to tell him to quit. The last time she tried, they fought. Brad had reminded her she wanted the high-profile job, the generous salary, the lifestyle it bought. He said she’d gotten what she asked for, and now he had to keep delivering. So she stayed quiet and watched their marriage fray.

Then one evening he came home at six-thirty, eyes bright like the man she’d married. He stretched, tucked a lock of her hair behind her ear, and announced, “I’m going to quit!” Over dinner he explained he would work for himself, from home. Confidence and boyish charm flowed back into him, and she believed him. That night they made love. As Cathy fell asleep, she half expected morning to take it all away.

It didn’t, not exactly. Brad set up in the spare room and threw himself into his “solo venture.” He worked long hours and asked Cathy not to disturb him. He promised it was only until things got rolling. She wanted to trust him, and he did seem happier, even excited. But a month passed and he was still glued to the computer from dawn to late night. Cathy felt shut out of a growing part of his life. Curiosity curdled into worry.

She crept to his office door, listening: steady typing, then soft laughter. More keys, another stifled laugh. Finance wasn’t exactly a comedy, she thought. The sound didn’t fit the story he was telling her. A fear took shape: was he talking to someone else? She resolved to find out.

Later, a shout cracked the quiet. “Dammit!” Brad slammed his hand on the desk and stormed down the hall. The office door flew open and Cathy, startled, nearly stumbled in. He rushed past to the kitchen. The computer was still on. Temptation won. She hurried to the desk and read the open chat. When Brad returned, she read aloud, mocking and shaking: “I don’t want to do this anymore. It’s over. It’s been fun but our sweet affair is over. Goodbye. Charlotte.”

She turned. “Was ‘Charlotte’ her real name? What did she call you, honey?” Brad shrugged, eyes on the floor. “How long?” Silence. “How long, Brad?” He muttered that it had started the very day he came home and said he’d quit. The confession broke something. Cathy dragged the monitor off the desk. It crashed to the floor. She called him pathetic and slapped him.

Anger and betrayal twisted everything. Cathy’s reaction was wrong; violence never fixes what lies and secrecy break. Still shaking, she demanded, “Have you earned any money?” He shook his head. “I think you should go back to a regular office job. And stay off the internet. Okay?” “Okay, babe,” he said.

They stood in the wrecked silence, both complicit in the distance that had opened between them: her pressure and silence, his withdrawal and “just talking” that wasn’t innocent. The work, the money, the screen—none of it had been worth what they were losing.
